citations.narrative:
The acquisition and disposition records available for review were not current or complete. The facility did not have the
address of the individual who they acquired 2 emu from or the date of disposition of 2 pigs. An incomplete record of
acquisitions and dispositions does not allow for proper traceability of regulated animals.
Complete records are needed for proper management of the regulated animals at a facility and those being brokered,
acquired, held, and sold. Every exhibitor shall make, keep, and maintain records or forms which fully and correctly
disclose information concerning animals other than dogs and cats, purchased or otherwise acquired, owned, held, leased,
or otherwise in his or her possession or under his or her control, or which is transported, sold, euthanized, or otherwise
disposed of by that dealer or exhibitor. The records shall include any offspring born or hatched of any animal while in his
or her possession or under his or her control. Correct by ensuring the acquisition and disposition records concerning all
regulated animals are current and complete. Correct by 10-MARCH-2026.

The outdoor housing facilities are not being adequately maintained in good repair and do not protect the animals from
injury. A primary enclosure currently housing 2 red foxes contains an elevated resting platform constructed of wood that
was observed to have multiple holes. The holes measured approximately 2-6 inches in length. The enclosure also
contains a wood ramp that was missing a large portion of wood from a sideboard, weakening the structural integrity of the
ramp.
Failure to maintain the housing facilities in good repair or replace damaged structures could result in injury to the animals
being housed in these enclosures. Correct by ensuring outdoor housing facilities are structurally sound and maintained in
good repair to protect the animals from injury. Correct by 10-MARCH-2026.

Supplies of food are not being stored in a manner to protect them from contamination. The facility utilizes a covered
structure to store the hay used to feed the animals, including zebra and zebra/horse hybrid. This structure is located in a
paddock that previously housed animals. In the back of this structure, the ground underneath and surrounding the hay
bales was observed to contain too numerous to count pieces of dried manure from previous resident animals.
Exposure to fecal material could contaminate the feed with microorganisms and parasites leading to illness or disease
transmission to the animals. The presence of excreta in the storage area also could also attract unwanted vermin and
pests that could further contaminate the food supplies. Correct by ensuring that supplies of food are stored in a manner
that adequately protects such supplies against deterioration, molding, or contamination by vermin. Correct by 10-MARCH-
2026.

The facility is not adequately cleaning enclosures. In the barn, approximately three stalls used to house zebras contained
an excessive accumulation of excreta on the walls. The excreta was thick, brown/black in color, and is located primarily
on the back and lower sections of each stall. Accumulations of excreta can lead to contamination of the animals, may lead
to spread of disease, and create odors. Excreta shall be removed from primary enclosures as often as necessary to
prevent contamination of the animals contained therein and to minimize disease hazards and to reduce odors. Correct by
ensuring that excreta is removed as often as necessary to prevent accumulation that may affect the animals. Correct by
10-MARCH-2026.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the facility representative.
Additional Inspectors:
Eric Irvin, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER n

narrative:
2.75(b)(1) 
   
Records: Dealers and exhibitors. 
The acquisition and disposition records available for review were not current or complete. The facility did not have the 
address of the individual who they acquired 2 emu from or the date of disposition of 2 pigs. An incomplete record of 
acquisitions and dispositions does not allow for proper traceability of regulated animals.

or otherwise in his or her possession or under his or her control, or which is transported, sold, euthanized, or otherwise

or her possession or under his or her control. Correct by ensuring the acquisition and disposition records concerning all 
regulated animals are current and complete. Correct by 10-MARCH-2026. 
3.125(a)

The outdoor housing facilities are not being adequately maintained in good repair and do not protect the animals from

Failure to maintain the housing facilities in good repair or replace damaged structures could result in injury to the animals 
being housed in these enclosures. Correct by ensuring outdoor housing facilities are structurally sound and maintained in 
good repair to protect the animals from injury. Correct by 10-MARCH-2026. 
3.125(c) 
   
Facilities, general.

Supplies of food are not being stored in a manner to protect them from contamination. The facility utilizes a covered 
structure to store the hay used to feed the animals, including zebra and zebra/horse hybrid. This structure is located in a 
paddock that previously housed animals. In the back of this structure, the ground underneath and surrounding the hay

Exposure to fecal material could contaminate the feed with microorganisms and parasites leading to illness or disease

that adequately protects such supplies against deterioration, molding, or contamination by vermin. Correct by 10-MARCH-
2026. 
3.131(a) 
   
Sanitation. 
The facility is not adequately cleaning enclosures. In the barn, approximately three stalls used to house zebras contained 
an excessive accumulation of excreta on the walls. The excreta was thick, brown/black in color, and is located primarily 
on the back and lower sections of each stall. Accumulations of excreta can lead to contamination of the animals, may lead 
to spread of disease, and create odors. Excreta shall be removed from primary enclosures as often as necessary to 
prevent contamination of the animals contained therein and to minimize disease hazards and to reduce odors. Correct by 
ensuring that excreta is removed as often as necessary to prevent accumulation that may affect the animals. Correct by 
10-MARCH-2026. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the facility representative. 
 
Additional Inspectors: 
Eric Irvin, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER n
